Global Sanitation Access: A Crisis Hidden in Plain Sight

Author

Aryan Rishi

Published

April 13, 2026

Code 
# Load raw files using Polars
indicator_raw = pl.read_csv("unicef_indicator_1.csv")
metadata_raw  = pl.read_csv("unicef_metadata.csv", infer_schema_length=5000)

# Filter to Total sex rows only to avoid double counting
indicator = (
    indicator_raw
    .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")
    .select(["country", "alpha_3_code", "time_period", "obs_value"])
    .rename({"obs_value": "sanitation_pct", "time_period": "year"})
)

# Select useful metadata columns and rename
metadata = (
    metadata_raw
    .select([
        "country", "alpha_3_code", "year",
        "Population, total",
        "GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$)",
        "Life expectancy at birth, total (years)",
    ])
    .rename({
        "Population, total":                       "population",
        "GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$)":      "gdp_per_capita",
        "Life expectancy at birth, total (years)": "life_expectancy",
    })
)

# Join indicator data with metadata on country code and year
merged = indicator.join(metadata, on=["alpha_3_code", "year"], how="left")

# Get the latest available data point per country
latest = (
    merged
    .group_by("alpha_3_code")
    .agg([
        pl.col("country").first(),
        pl.col("year").max().alias("latest_year"),
        pl.col("sanitation_pct").last().alias("sanitation_pct"),
        pl.col("gdp_per_capita").last().alias("gdp_per_capita"),
        pl.col("life_expectancy").last().alias("life_expectancy"),
        pl.col("population").last().alias("population"),
    ])
    .drop_nulls("sanitation_pct")
)

# Assign regional groupings manually
region_map = {
    "AFG":"South Asia","BGD":"South Asia","IND":"South Asia","NPL":"South Asia",
    "PAK":"South Asia","LKA":"South Asia","BTN":"South Asia","MDV":"South Asia",
    "NGA":"Sub-Saharan Africa","ETH":"Sub-Saharan Africa","COD":"Sub-Saharan Africa",
    "TZA":"Sub-Saharan Africa","KEN":"Sub-Saharan Africa","MOZ":"Sub-Saharan Africa",
    "GHA":"Sub-Saharan Africa","UGA":"Sub-Saharan Africa","ZMB":"Sub-Saharan Africa",
    "MDG":"Sub-Saharan Africa","MWI":"Sub-Saharan Africa","SEN":"Sub-Saharan Africa",
    "ZWE":"Sub-Saharan Africa","CMR":"Sub-Saharan Africa","CIV":"Sub-Saharan Africa",
    "NER":"Sub-Saharan Africa","MLI":"Sub-Saharan Africa","BFA":"Sub-Saharan Africa",
    "GIN":"Sub-Saharan Africa","RWA":"Sub-Saharan Africa","SSD":"Sub-Saharan Africa",
    "CHN":"East Asia & Pacific","IDN":"East Asia & Pacific","PHL":"East Asia & Pacific",
    "VNM":"East Asia & Pacific","MMR":"East Asia & Pacific","KHM":"East Asia & Pacific",
    "LAO":"East Asia & Pacific","PNG":"East Asia & Pacific","FJI":"East Asia & Pacific",
    "BRA":"Latin America","MEX":"Latin America","COL":"Latin America","ARG":"Latin America",
    "PER":"Latin America","BOL":"Latin America","HTI":"Latin America","GTM":"Latin America",
    "HND":"Latin America","NIC":"Latin America","DOM":"Latin America",
    """USA""":"High Income","GBR":"High Income","DEU":"High Income","FRA":"High Income",
    "JPN":"High Income","AUS":"High Income","CAN":"High Income","NOR":"High Income",
    "SWE":"High Income","CHE":"High Income","NLD":"High Income",
    "EGY":"Middle East & N. Africa","MAR":"Middle East & N. Africa",
    "DZA":"Middle East & N. Africa","IRQ":"Middle East & N. Africa",
    "YEM":"Middle East & N. Africa","SYR":"Middle East & N. Africa",
}

region_pl = pl.DataFrame({
    "alpha_3_code": list(region_map.keys()),
    "region":       list(region_map.values())
})

latest = latest.join(region_pl, on="alpha_3_code", how="left").with_columns(
    pl.col("region").fill_null("Other")
)

# Convert to pandas for plotnine compatibility
latest_pd = latest.to_pandas()
merged_pd = merged.to_pandas()

print(f"Countries in snapshot : {len(latest_pd)}")
print(f"Total rows in dataset : {len(indicator)}")
print(latest_pd[["country","sanitation_pct","gdp_per_capita","life_expectancy"]].describe().round(1))
Countries in snapshot : 225
Total rows in dataset : 5436
       sanitation_pct  gdp_per_capita  life_expectancy
count           225.0           188.0             19.0
mean             88.1         17876.0             71.0
std              19.3         27445.5              5.4
min              18.7           268.7             61.5
25%              88.0          2210.0             66.1
50%              98.2          6800.1             71.4
75%              99.9         21516.3             74.9
max             100.0        247170.2             81.8

Introduction

Access to basic sanitation — toilets, latrines, and sewage systems — is enshrined as UN Sustainable Development Goal 6. Yet billions of people still lack it today.

This report analyses UNICEF data on the proportion of people with access to improved sanitation facilities across countries and over time. The findings are stark:

  • Countries in Sub-Saharan Africa have average sanitation coverage below 40%
  • There is a strong positive correlation between national wealth and sanitation access
  • Progress since 2000 has been real but uneven across regions
  • Low sanitation consistently accompanies lower life expectancy, confirming this is a public health emergency

“Sanitation is more important than independence.” — Mahatma Gandhi

Chart 2 — Bar Chart: The 20 Worst-Served Countries

Code 
bottom20 = (
    latest_pd
    .nsmallest(20, "sanitation_pct")
    .sort_values("sanitation_pct")
)
bottom20["country_label"] = bottom20["country"].str[:22]

p_bar = (
    ggplot(bottom20, aes(x="reorder(country_label, sanitation_pct)", y="sanitation_pct"))
    + geom_col(fill=BLUE_MID, color=BLUE_DARK, width=0.7)
    + geom_text(
        aes(label="sanitation_pct.round(1).astype(str) + '%' "),
        ha="left", size=7, color=BLUE_DARK, nudge_y=0.8
    )
    + geom_hline(yintercept=50, linetype="dashed", color="red", alpha=0.6, size=0.8)
    + coord_flip()
    + scale_y_continuous(limits=(0, 58), labels=lambda l: [f"{v:.0f}%" for v in l])
    + labs(
        title="20 Countries with Lowest Sanitation Access",
        subtitle="Red dashed line = 50% threshold  |  Every country here falls below it",
        x="Country",
        y="Population with Improved Sanitation (%)",
        caption="Source: UNICEF indicator data"
    )
    + blue_theme()
)

p_bar

Every country shown here sits below the 50% mark. Nations such as South Sudan, Chad, and Niger report figures below 20%, meaning four out of five people have no access to an improved sanitation facility. This is both a dignity crisis and a driver of preventable disease.

Chart 3 — Scatterplot: Wealth vs. Sanitation Access

Code 
scatter_df = (
    latest_pd
    .dropna(subset=["sanitation_pct", "gdp_per_capita"])
    .query("gdp_per_capita < 80000")
)

region_colors = {
    "Sub-Saharan Africa":     "#1565c0",
    "South Asia":             "#42a5f5",
    "East Asia & Pacific":    "#0288d1",
    "Latin America":          "#26c6da",
    "Middle East & N. Africa":"#5c6bc0",
    "High Income":            "#81d4fa",
    "Other":                  "#cfd8dc",
}

p_scatter = (
    ggplot(scatter_df, aes(x="gdp_per_capita", y="sanitation_pct", color="region"))
    + geom_point(alpha=0.75, size=2.5)
    + geom_smooth(
        aes(x="gdp_per_capita", y="sanitation_pct"),
        method="lm", color="red", se=True, inherit_aes=False, size=1
    )
    + scale_color_manual(values=region_colors, name="Region")
    + scale_x_continuous(labels=lambda l: [f"${v/1000:.0f}k" for v in l])
    + scale_y_continuous(labels=lambda l: [f"{v:.0f}%" for v in l])
    + labs(
        title="Sanitation Access vs. GDP per Capita",
        subtitle="Wealthier countries generally have higher coverage — but outliers show policy matters too",
        x="GDP per Capita (constant 2015 USD)",
        y="Population with Improved Sanitation (%)",
        caption="Source: UNICEF + World Bank metadata"
    )
    + blue_theme()
)

p_scatter

The trend is clear: as GDP rises, sanitation access improves. However, the scatter shows important outliers. Some middle-income countries in Latin America and East Asia have achieved 80–90% coverage at relatively modest income levels, demonstrating that investment choices and governance matter as much as raw wealth. The poorest nations, clustered in the bottom-left, face a compounding trap — low income and low sanitation feed each other.

Chart 4 — Time Series: Regional Progress Since 2000

Code 
merged_region = merged_pd.merge(
    latest_pd[["alpha_3_code", "region"]],
    on="alpha_3_code", how="left"
)

ts_df = (
    merged_region
    .dropna(subset=["sanitation_pct", "region"])
    .query("year >= 2000")
    .groupby(["year", "region"], as_index=False)["sanitation_pct"]
    .mean()
    .query("region != 'Other'")
)

region_line_colors = {
    "Sub-Saharan Africa":     "#1565c0",
    "South Asia":             "#42a5f5",
    "East Asia & Pacific":    "#0288d1",
    "Latin America":          "#26c6da",
    "Middle East & N. Africa":"#5c6bc0",
    "High Income":            "#81d4fa",
}

p_ts = (
    ggplot(ts_df, aes(x="year", y="sanitation_pct", color="region"))
    + geom_line(size=1.2)
    + geom_point(size=1.5, alpha=0.6)
    + scale_color_manual(values=region_line_colors, name="Region")
    + scale_y_continuous(labels=lambda l: [f"{v:.0f}%" for v in l])
    + labs(
        title="Sanitation Access by Region: 2000–2022",
        subtitle="Average % of population with access to improved sanitation facilities",
        x="Year",
        y="Average Sanitation Access (%)",
        caption="Source: UNICEF indicator data"
    )
    + blue_theme()
)

p_ts

Every region has improved since 2000 — this is real progress. South Asia shows the steepest gains, driven largely by national campaigns in India and Bangladesh. Sub-Saharan Africa has grown steadily but remains far behind, averaging below 50% in 2022. High-income countries are effectively flat near 99%, having solved this problem decades ago. The trajectory for the poorest regions, if continued, will not reach universal coverage by 2030 — the SDG deadline.

Conclusion

The UNICEF sanitation data tells a human story behind the numbers:

  • Where you are born determines whether you have a toilet — geography and income are the dominant predictors of access.
  • Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia carry the heaviest burden, home to the majority of those without access.
  • Wealth explains much, but not everything — targeted policy and investment can accelerate progress beyond what income alone predicts.
  • The clock is running out on SDG 6. At current rates of improvement, universal basic sanitation will not be achieved by 2030.

Sanitation is not a development footnote. It prevents cholera, typhoid, and childhood diarrhoea — the leading killers of under-fives in low-income countries. Every data point on these charts is a community of real people.

The world has the resources. What is missing is urgency.

Report created using Python (Polars, plotnine) and rendered with Quarto. Data: UNICEF.
